main_summary.summary: Ellie Dahmer, widow of Vernon Dahmer Sr., recounts her life in the Kelly Settlement of Forrest County, Mississippi, and her family's central role in voter-registration organizing in Hattiesburg. Trained at Alcorn and Tennessee A&I, she earned a bachelor's degree in home economics in 1947 and taught in Jasper, Jones, and Forrest Counties before being blacklisted following school consolidation at Earl Travillion. She describes meeting Vernon Dahmer, raising their children on the family farm and sawmill, and trying repeatedly to register to vote with Forrest County registrar Theron Lynd, who refused to issue cards to qualified Black applicants. Ellie was one of the 18 plaintiffs in U.S. v. Lynd, the Justice Department case argued by John Doar; her husband's voter-registration card arrived only in time to be buried with him after the KKK firebombing of their home on 10 January 1966. She hosted NAACP meetings in her living room, sheltered SNCC organizers Curtis Hayes (Curtis Muhammad) and Hollis Watkins from McComb, cooked for Freedom Summer 1964 volunteers, and later served three terms as a Forrest County election commissioner.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Ellie Dahmer is a first-person witness to the Forrest County voter-registration campaign at its center: she names Theron Lynd's literacy-test procedure, the affidavit notarization required of Black teachers who gave money to the NAACP, and the U.S. v. Lynd trial evidence that two Black women who appeared white were registered without testing, proving Lynd's disparate-treatment intent. She is also the sole surviving narrator of life inside the Dahmer household during the months leading to the January 1966 firebombing, including the family's practice of sleeping in shifts and her own work sewing blackout curtains.

The interview documents the systematic draft-targeting of NAACP-family sons (her testimony that Alvin was drafted on crutches), the role of the Kelly Settlement as a hosting site for SNCC organizers Curtis Hayes and Hollis Watkins from McComb, and the Freedom Summer 1964 watermelon-picnic incident in which a young SNCC tractor-driver inadvertently rode through the white side of Hattiesburg. Her later service as a Forrest County election commissioner gives the interview a post-Movement closing arc tied directly to the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

chapters.title: Old as the Dean Himself
chapters.summary: Ellie Dahmer recalls two years at Alcorn before transferring to Tennessee A&I, where a former Alcorn dean threw out her credits and held her an extra quarter, saying her Alcorn education was as old as he was, then earning a home economics degree in 1947 and teaching for better salary across Jasper and Forrest counties in schools starved of new books and resources.

chapters.title: They Stayed for Dinner
chapters.topic: Meeting Vernon Dahmer in Kelly Settlement
chapters.summary: She describes coming to teach home economics in Kelly Settlement and first meeting Mr. Dahmer when he and several others came about the teaching job and stayed for the dinner she was cooking, and how after schools consolidated all Forrest County's Black students into Earl Travillion she was not rehired and took a job thirty miles away.

chapters.title: He Put Christianity Into Practice
chapters.topic: The Farm, the Sawmill, and the Store
chapters.summary: Ellie Dahmer remembers Vernon as a deeply Christian man concerned for his fellow man who built a comfortable living from the farm, sawmill, and grocery store, worked his own and his siblings' land with tractors and hired help, had the children driving and doing farm work very young, and raised six boys plus a daughter, Betty, who worked alongside them while Ellie cooked for everyone and kept the sales-tax paperwork.

chapters.title: Read That and Tell Me What It Means
chapters.topic: Registrar Lynd and the Constitution Test
chapters.summary: She recounts trying to register before the registrar Lynd, who handed her a card with part of the Constitution and told her she failed no matter how well she explained it, the requirement that teachers notarize which organizations they funded since giving money to the NAACP could get them fired, and Vernon's voter registration card arriving only in time to be buried with him.

chapters.part: From the Polls to the Office
chapters.title: Something They Had to Die For
chapters.summary: Ellie Dahmer describes serving three terms as election commissioner for District 2 some ten or fifteen years after Vernon was killed, gathering more than enough qualifying signatures with her daughter's help when another qualified woman declined to run against a white opponent, and the wonderful feeling of helping with something people had died for while making sure no one else was denied.

chapters.part: The NAACP in the Living Room
chapters.title: Meetings on the Same Spot
chapters.summary: She reflects that there were always some good white people though many were nasty, declining to name the harassing families, and tells of learning the NAACP was active after moving to the area, the meetings held in her own living room with a lookout posted, the day the chief of police drove into the yard, and the Beard family, the young Ladner sisters, and others active through the same church, some of whom had relatives passing for white in Chicago and California.

chapters.title: We Stopped Sleeping in Shifts
chapters.summary: Ellie Dahmer describes the threats and fear during the registration years, the strength she and Vernon drew from each other, his refusal to drive alone or stop for police he could not trust, always carrying a gun, keeping the porch light off, and the family sleeping in shifts and keeping watch while she replaced the curtains with blackout ones so no one could see in.

chapters.title: KKK Nailed to the Poles
chapters.summary: She recalls drive-by shootings at the mailbox, Ku Klux Klan signs nailed to the poles that Vernon would tear down, and her belief that the harassment came out of Jones County, where the sheriff and his relatives were all in it together, so calling the law was useless.

chapters.title: They Thought They Were White
chapters.summary: Ellie Dahmer tells how the case against registrar Lynd broke open when two light-skinned Black neighbors, including Erlene Beard's family, were let register without the Constitution test because officials assumed they were white, giving the proof needed, and how she was one of the eighteen plaintiffs Lynd was forced to register, attending court every day and meeting John Doar and a young photographer the three-judge panel threatened with jail.

chapters.title: Two Young Men in a Suitcase
chapters.topic: Hollis Watkins, Curtis Hayes, and Freedom Summer
chapters.summary: She recounts Vernon returning from a Jackson meeting with two young SNCC and COFO organizers out of McComb, Hollis Watkins and Curtis Hayes, who lived and worked with the family like their own children and canvassed for voter registration, and her own Freedom Summer role cooking for the volunteers, including a picnic and a watermelon run when a young man drove a tractor through the white community, while her older sons were drafted as soon as they turned eighteen.

chapters.title: A Booth Where They Sold Gas
chapters.topic: The Voting Rights Act and Federal Registrars
chapters.summary: Ellie Dahmer describes the wonderful feeling when the Voting Rights Act passed, and how Forrest County still kept Black people out of the courthouse, setting up federal registrars in a rented booth where gas had been sold, after which people began registering quickly.

loc_video.caption: Ellie Dahmer oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, 2015 November 30
